claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children that you'd like me to assess for Child Benefit eligibility?** If so, could you please tell me their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I appreciate you providing Alex's details.

Before I proceed with the assessment, I need to understand your situation a bit better. 

**Is Alex still in full-time education (such as at school, college, or university), or has he left education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's left education - he's not studying at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to gather a bit more information to make a proper assessment.

**Is Alex currently working, or is he unemployed and looking for work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's not working at the moment, no.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one more important question:

**Does Alex live with you, or does he live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Before I make my final assessment, let me check the eligibility rules to ensure I give you an accurate answer.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see from the rules that there's an important point about children who are 16 or over. Let me make sure I have all the information I need to give you an accurate answer.

Based on the eligibility rules I've reviewed, I need to ask one more clarifying question:

**After Alex left education, did you register him with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within 20 weeks of him leaving?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I didn't register him with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the UK government's Child Benefit eligibility rules, I can now provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex. 

Here's why: While you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and in approved education or training, Alex is 18 years old, has left education, and is not in training. 

There was a potential exception that could have helped – if Alex had registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within 20 weeks of leaving education, you could have continued to claim for a further 20 weeks. However, as he was not registered during that timeframe, this exception doesn't apply.

If you have any other questions or believe your circumstances may have changed, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further advice.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and no longer in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. There is an exception that allows you to apply for a 20-week extension if a 16 or 17-year-old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex is now 18 and you did not register him with a careers service or the armed services within the required timeframe when he left education. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex lives with you, he is 18 years old, has left education, is not working, and was not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within 20 weeks of leaving education. Child Benefit for children aged 16 and over is only payable if they remain in approved education or training, or if they registered with a careers service or armed services within the specific timeframe. None of these conditions apply to Alex's situation."}

However, Alex is now 18 and you did not register him with a careers service or the armed services within the required timeframe when he left education. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
